description: A $99 self-paced program preparing international entrepreneurs and CEOs to launch and operate a business in the United States, with a focus on the Central Florida ecosystem. Covers legal setup, taxes, banking, IP protection, hiring/immigration, go-to-market strategy, U.S. business culture, and the Central Florida support network. Designed for vetted founders from Latin America, Europe, and Asia who are expanding to or have recently relocated to the U.S.

personas.name: The Soft Lander
personas.description: Founder or executive of an established company outside the U.S. — most commonly Latin America or Europe. The business already works in their home country; they're expanding into the U.S. and evaluating Central Florida as a base.
goals: Understand what to do and in what order — legal setup, banking, finding the right people
Establish a credible U.S. presence from abroad
Connect with local resources for soft landing support
painPoints: Overwhelmed by sequential requirements (entity → EIN → bank → address)
Doesn't know what they don't know about U.S. regulations
Needs a physical U.S. presence but isn't ready to relocate full-time

personas.name: The Latin American Entrepreneur
personas.description: Entrepreneur from a Spanish-speaking country (Venezuela, Colombia, Mexico, Brazil) expanding from abroad or recently relocated. Deeply entrepreneurial, but U.S. banking, legal structures, and professional norms are unfamiliar.
goals: Navigate legal and financial systems in a second language
Build a credible professional network in Central Florida
Access Spanish-language resources and community support
painPoints: Banking system rejections and credit history barriers
Cultural differences in business communication and trust-building

personas.name: The Relocated Founder
personas.description: International entrepreneur who has physically moved to the U.S. — often on an O-1, E-2, or similar visa. Technically strong and motivated, but overwhelmed by the administrative side of building a business as a non-citizen.
goals: Protect intellectual property in the U.S. legal system
Acquire first U.S. customers and understand local sales cycles
painPoints: Understanding U.S. customer acquisition expectations and pricing

primary: International founders and CEOs expanding to the U.S. market
Latin American entrepreneurs entering Central Florida
Relocated founders on O-1, E-2, or L-1 visas
secondary: UCF BIP Soft Landing program candidates
International business development professionals exploring U.S. operations
targetAudience.prerequisites: Active business in home country or concrete plans to establish a U.S. entity. Vetted by UCF BIP before enrollment.
targetAudience.experienceLevel: Experienced business operators, new to U.S. market

learningObjectives.terminal: After completing this program, the learner will be able to make informed, risk-aware decisions about establishing and operating a business in the United States, with specific knowledge of the Central Florida ecosystem and the resources available through UCF BIP.
enabling: Compare LLC and C-Corp structures for international founders, understand why S-Corp is generally not available to non-citizens, and identify which entity type fits their business goals and immigration status
Describe the step-by-step process for registering a Florida business through SunBiz, obtaining an EIN, and establishing a registered address
Understand U.S. federal/state taxes, sales tax, and basic accounting expectations
Navigate U.S. banking requirements, document expectations, and common pitfalls for foreign founders
Protect intellectual property, manage contracts, and determine when legal counsel is required
Understand U.S. hiring practices, contractor vs. employee distinctions, visa options, and payroll obligations
Apply U.S. customer acquisition strategies, pricing expectations, and market entry approaches to their specific business context
Understand U.S. business cultural norms and avoid costly missteps
Identify Central Florida business support resources that are available to help settle in and achieve your business goals
Assess whether U.S. market expansion is realistic for their company and business stage
Produce actionable deliverables including checklists, a Florida setup roadmap, and a U.S. market readiness assessment

notes.outcome7-customer-acquisition: Keep for now but first to cut if total duration needs trimming (Brian's C3)
notes.outcome11-deliverables: Confirmed — checklist, roadmap, resource guide, worksheet as Module 8 downloads

instructionalApproach.methodology: Self-paced, non-linear modular learning with practical checklists, cost-range tables, and real-world founder examples. Framed as 'what you need to know before you spend money' — risk reduction, not academic theory.
engagement: Common Mistake callout boxes highlighting real founder errors
Before You Spend Money checklists for actionable preparation
Estimated Cost Range tables with 2025-2026 data
Typical Timeline charts showing realistic expectations
Reality Check callouts contrasting expectations vs. reality
Real-world founder stories from international entrepreneurs
Interactive comparisons, timelines, scenarios, and accordions
Latin America supplement sections with region-specific resources
Central Florida Fun Facts — Easter egg-style discoverable facts tied to each module's landscape theme (economic engine data, unique regional facts)
Landscape theme integration — each module's content weaves in its Central Florida landscape analogy throughout, not just as a sidebar

assessmentStrategy.formative: Three-part end-of-module template: (1) Reflection prompts — think about your own business situation, (2) Scenario-based multiple choice — 'what would you do?' not trivia, (3) Key Takeaway summary. Consistent format across all modules.
assessmentStrategy.summative: End-of-course self-assessment reflection in Wrap-Up module. Value perception feedback (strategic phrasing for stakeholder data). Program improvement feedback (separate).
assessmentStrategy.practiceOpportunities: Scenario-based decision exercises, comparison card interactions, resource matching activities

checkForUnderstandingTemplate.description: Standardized end-of-module format agreed with Brian (Mar 18 meeting). Applied consistently to every module.

part1_reflection.format: 3 reflection prompts tailored to the module topic
examplePrompts: What am I most unsure about in this area?
What might I be overlooking that could affect my business?
What risks do I need to watch for?
part1_reflection.tone: Invite the founder to think about their own situation, not recall facts

part2_scenarioCheck.style: Situational — 'You are about to do X, how should you approach it?' NOT trivia ('Is it 1 month or 3 months?')
part2_scenarioCheck.explanationTone: Connect answer back to the module's key insight

part3_keyTakeaway.format: Conversational summary of the module's main message
elements: Highlight the strategy or framework to remember
Remind this is a map, not a guarantee
Encourage use of reflections to track progress

theme.typography: Clean sans-serif, high readability, adequate spacing
theme.mood: Professional, trustworthy, practical — knowledgeable friend, not professor

designNotes.branding: UCF BIP branding. Navy primary, gold accent, white backgrounds. Driving/road/GPS metaphor for navigation. Modern technical HUD dashboard aesthetic with Central Florida imagery.
designNotes.visualStyle: Stylized but not cartoonish, not photorealistic. Somewhere between — think digital painting or Syd Mead-inspired futurism. Confirmed by Brian (Mar 18).
sensitivityNotes: Practical, not academic — 'I wish someone had told me this' framing throughout
Respect diverse cultural backgrounds — Latin America, Europe, Asia represented
Legal/tax/immigration content always includes 'consult a professional' disclaimer
No cultural stereotypes — use specific, actionable examples
Frame UCF BIP programs naturally, not as sales pitch
Flesch-Kincaid grade 8-10 for non-native English speakers
Avoid anything controversial, brand-specific (no Disney, Universal by name), or region-negative
Landscapes are inspirational, not literal — 'inspired by Cape Canaveral' not 'this is Cape Canaveral'

centralFloridaLandscapeThemes.description: Each module is associated with a Central Florida landscape that serves as visual backdrop, analogy source, and fun-fact anchor. Landscapes are inspirational/generic — not literal depictions of specific locations.

module-0.notes: GPS/car dashboard POV — the starting point of the journey

module-1.notes: Government, business formation, downtown Orlando economic engine

module-3.notes: Science, Cape Canaveral, innovation, protecting what you build

module-4.notes: Teamwork, talent, the massive employment/operations engine

module-5.notes: Commerce, reaching customers, retail and logistics hub

module-6.notes: How business works — casual surface, serious underneath

module-7.notes: Ecosystem — 'you are not alone', full natural ecosystem analogy, alligators and all

module-8.notes: Wrap-up, reflection, next steps — return to base

funFacts.description: Each module includes 1-2 discoverable fun facts about Central Florida tied to the landscape theme. Gamification element — finding all facts requires consuming all content.
funFacts.visibility: Obvious, not hidden. Brian wants them visible but optionally expandable. 'When you see the UCF logo, click for fun facts' as fallback. Ideally woven into the content.
examples: Theme parks generate $75+ billion annually in Florida's economy
Cape Canaveral area has one of the highest concentrations of aerospace engineers in the world
Central Florida has the largest concentration of outlet shopping in the state
Port Canaveral is one of the busiest cruise ports in the world

calloutTypes.label: Common Mistakes International Founders Make
calloutTypes.notes: Repeating callout — every module gets one. Brian provided examples: Mod1 (wrong entity, personal bank accounts, ignoring registered agent), Mod2 (ignoring sales tax, waiting too long for accountant). Generate analogous mistakes for each module.

calloutTypes.label: Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs
calloutTypes.notes: Repeating callout — every module gets one. Realistic cost ranges for that module's topic. Replaces inline cost numbers. No cost numbers in Module 0 welcome.

fromSource: Brian's 10 curated public source URLs (SBA, SunBiz, SelectFlorida, SBDC, etc.)
Pre-production brief with personas, module outline, and Brian's inline comments
March 5 meeting prep with confirmed module reorder and feature scope decisions
March 12 meeting transcript with content/design direction confirmations
User feedback from 4 international founders (preview prototype responses)
toGenerate: 25 lesson content JSON files (Welcome + 7 modules + Wrap-Up; Module 5 has 2, Module 7 has 4, Module 8 has 3)
Per-module Key Takeaways video scripts (7 modules)
Image prompts for 30-40 professional scene images

contentGenerationGuardRails.description: Rules applied during all content generation and regeneration. Established from Brian's Mar 18 feedback session.

toneGuidelines.eslFriendly: Write for non-native English speakers regardless of language version. Simple sentence structures, clear vocabulary, avoid idioms without explanation. Flesch-Kincaid grade 8-10.
toneGuidelines.conversationalInformal: Less academic, more practical. Like a knowledgeable friend, not a professor. Contractions welcome. Short paragraphs. Direct address ('you'). Avoid formal academic constructions.
toneGuidelines.notGimmicky: Themed content should be classy and appropriate. Reference Central Florida landscapes without going too far into roleplay or forced humor.

absoluteStatementPolicy.rule: NEVER use absolute statements about costs, timelines, or outcomes. Always qualify with 'typically', 'often', 'may vary', 'based on typical experiences', etc.

examples.wrong: It takes 2-3 days to register an LLC
examples.right: LLC registration is often completed within a few business days, though timelines may vary

absoluteStatementPolicy.rationale: Legal liability concern. Brian flagged this repeatedly — nothing should read as a promise or guarantee.
absoluteStatementPolicy.autoCheck: When regenerating content, scan for absolute language: 'will take', 'costs exactly', 'always', 'never', 'guaranteed', 'you should', 'you must' (unless citing a legal requirement).

liabilityCheck.rule: Scan all generated content for statements that could be interpreted as legal, tax, immigration, or financial advice. Add appropriate disclaimers.
liabilityCheck.directiveLanguage: Avoid 'you should do X' or 'this is how long it will take'. Use 'many founders find', 'a common approach is', 'consider consulting a professional about'.

sCorporationNote.rule: S-Corp is generally NOT available to non-citizen international founders (non-resident alien shareholders prohibited). Content should focus on LLC and C-Corp as primary options, with S-Corp mentioned only as 'probably not a fit for most international founders' with brief explanation of why.
sCorporationNote.flaggedBy: Brian (Mar 18) — spent time researching this and confirmed S-Corp irrelevance for target audience

consistencyAudit.rule: After editing any module, check for consistency in: tone, volume/duration relative to original, terminology, and disclaimer presence.
consistencyAudit.brian_note: Brian asked: 'Is there a way to audit for consistency after I edit?' — build this into regeneration process.

calloutBalance.rule: No lesson should have more than 3 callout blocks. If a lesson needs both 'Before You Spend Money' and 'Watch Your Money', merge them — they serve overlapping purposes. The teaching-to-callout ratio must stay healthy: callouts supplement content, they do not replace it.
exceptions: lesson-0-1 may exceed 3 due to mandatory legal and immigration disclaimers required by Brian

brandMentionPolicy.rule: Third-party brand names (Mercury, Relay Financial, Stripe, etc.) should appear at most ONCE per lesson in the main text. A second mention in a callout is acceptable only if it serves a distinct purpose. Three or more mentions of the same brand in a single lesson reads as endorsement and must be reduced.
brandMentionPolicy.rationale: Brian flagged: avoid being promotional. Mentioning alternatives is helpful; repeating them is endorsement.

contentDepthProportionality.rule: Allocate content depth proportionally to topic difficulty for the target audience. Topics that founders report as hardest or most frustrating (banking, taxes, cultural missteps) should receive MORE content depth, not less. Do not give an easy topic (filing on SunBiz) more word count than a hard topic (opening a bank account).
contentDepthProportionality.implementation: Before generating a module, rank the lessons by expected difficulty for an international founder. The hardest lesson should have the most teaching content (text blocks), not just the most callouts.

founderAnecdotes.rule: Every module must include at least 2 brief founder anecdotes — specific, plausible stories that illustrate a key point. These create the 'knowledgeable friend' voice that generic advice cannot. Anecdotes should include: country of origin, the specific situation, and the outcome or lesson learned.
founderAnecdotes.distribution: Spread across lessons within the module, not all in one lesson.

decisions.programPurpose: Rewritten by Brian to outcome-driven framing. Accepted.
decisions.objectivesToOutcomes: Changed 'Learning Objectives' to 'Learning Outcomes' throughout. Outcome language is action/result-focused, not content-descriptive.
decisions.costNumberPolicy: No specific dollar amounts in Module 0 welcome. All costs appear in per-module 'Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs' callouts.
